channel.title: NPR Topics: Health
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: This Ebola outbreak raises questions about when it all began — and the U.S. response
item.description: The sheer number of cases and deaths are a sign that the outbreak might have been smoldering before the virus was identified.
item.content:encoded:
The sheer number of cases and deaths are a sign that the outbreak might have been smoldering before the virus was identified.

(Image credit: Badru Katumba/AFP)
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Lambert

item.title: Thousands of U.S. countertop workers could have damaged lungs, safety expert says
item.description: Over 550 men in California have fallen ill after cutting natural or factory-made stone countertops. But epidemiologists say this isn't just a California problem.
item.content:encoded:
Over 550 men in California have fallen ill after cutting natural or factory-made stone countertops. But epidemiologists say this isn't just a California problem.

(Image credit: Tina Russell for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Nell Greenfieldboyce

item.title: The 'fibermaxxing' trend has health benefits worth the hype
item.description: There are a lot of wellness trends that make health experts roll their eyes in skepticism. But there is one big trend that many experts can get behind – with a few caveats: fibermaxxing.
item.content:encoded:
There are a lot of wellness trends that make health experts roll their eyes in skepticism. But there is one big trend that many experts can get behind – with a few caveats: fibermaxxing.

(Image credit: Natalia Gdovskaia)
item.dc:creator: Maria Godoy

item.title: When times are hard, we eat more beans. And it's happening again now
item.description: The fiber craze is pushing more people into the broad world of beans, as the U.S. bean industry looks to double American consumption of pulses by 2030.
item.content:encoded: The fiber craze is pushing more people into the broad world of beans, as the U.S. bean industry looks to double American consumption of pulses by 2030.
item.dc:creator: Joe Hernandez

item.title: PCOS has a new name. Can it help patients get better care?
item.description: Polycystic ovary syndrome is getting a new name. NPR's Ayesha Rascoe speaks to Dr. Melanie Cree, of Children's Hospital Colorado, about how the new name might help patients get better treatment.
item.content:encoded: Polycystic ovary syndrome is getting a new name. NPR's Ayesha Rascoe speaks to Dr. Melanie Cree, of Children's Hospital Colorado, about how the new name might help patients get better treatment.
item.dc:creator: Ayesha Rascoe

item.title: One clinic tracks the heavy toll Trump's immigration crackdown takes on mental health
item.description: Zocalo Health, a primary care organization, screens all its patients for depression, anxiety and suicidal thoughts. It documented a marked increase in those conditions since ICE enforcement actions began.
item.content:encoded:
Zocalo Health, a primary care organization, screens all its patients for depression, anxiety and suicidal thoughts. It documented a marked increase in those conditions since ICE enforcement actions began.

(Image credit: Michael M. Santiago)
item.dc:creator: Rhitu Chatterjee

item.title: World Health Organization declares Ebola outbreak in Congo a global health emergency
item.description: Health officials are working to contain a new Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo. There's no approved vaccine for the strain of the virus that's spreading.
item.content:encoded:
Health officials are working to contain a new Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo. There's no approved vaccine for the strain of the virus that's spreading.

(Image credit: Constant Same Bagalwa)
item.dc:creator: Sydney Lupkin

item.title: Elephants eat their crops. Farmers strike back. It's a war that's only getting worse
item.description: In Sri Lanka, the islanders revere elephants. But for farmers, there's rising tension that's leading to more fatal encounters — for both humans and hungry pachyderms.
item.content:encoded:
In Sri Lanka, the islanders revere elephants. But for farmers, there's rising tension that's leading to more fatal encounters — for both humans and hungry pachyderms.

(Image credit: Brent Stirton/Getty Images/Reportage Archive)
item.dc:creator: Diaa Hadid

item.title: Hantavirus countdown: U.S. cruise passengers settle in for 42 days of waiting
item.description: The hantavirus outbreak on a cruise ship has potentially exposed passengers to a deadly disease. Most returning Americans are now housed in Nebraska. Some may be cleared to quarantine at home.
item.content:encoded:
The hantavirus outbreak on a cruise ship has potentially exposed passengers to a deadly disease. Most returning Americans are now housed in Nebraska. Some may be cleared to quarantine at home. 

(Image credit: Dylan Widger)
item.dc:creator: Pien Huang

item.title: Son reflects with his mother about growing up with autism
item.description: Jhovana Figueroa was diagnosed with autism when he was a toddler. For StoryCorps, Figueroa and his mom talk about his childhood and their hopes for the future.
item.content:encoded: Jhovana Figueroa was diagnosed with autism when he was a toddler. For StoryCorps, Figueroa and his mom talk about his childhood and their hopes for the future.
item.dc:creator: Esther Honig
